death.cause: died in sleep at home, 1000 Mason St, after seven-year illness

burial.plot_notes: Funeral Grace Cathedral, San Francisco, November 23, 1932

parents.father_name: Benjamin Barnard Redding (Sacramento mayor; California Secretary of State; Central Pacific Railroad land agent)
parents.mother_name: Mary Prescott Putnam Redding (1820 Boston - ?; Israel Putnam descendant)
parents.father_research_notes: Built as easter_egg X02774 in Stage 6V.
parents.mother_research_notes: Built as easter_egg X02779 in Stage 6X-10.

education.notes: Studied piano under Hugo L. Mansfeldt; gave first public concerts (piano duos with Mansfeldt) at age 11.
Entered 1871; graduated 1874. Began playing serious chess while a student here.
Entered 1877; graduated 1879. Directed Harvard's college orchestra; won the school's billiards tournament.

career.title: Attorney, Hall McAllister
career.organization: Hall McAllister legal firm, San Francisco
career.notes: Joined the firm at age 21 immediately after Harvard Law graduation.

career.title: Land-departments attorney
career.organization: Southern Pacific Transportation Co. + Central Pacific Railroad
career.notes: Argued Central Pacific Railroad v. Mudd (1881), CP v. Shackelford (1883), Southern Pacific Railroad Co. v. Dull (1884), SP v. Poole (1887), among other railroad-industry cases. Same organizations for which his father Benjamin had served as land agent.

career.title: Argued United States v. Kagama before the US Supreme Court
career.notes: At age 27 represented Kagama, a Yurok Native American accused of murder. Justice Samuel Freeman Miller's opinion UPHELD the constitutionality of the Major Crimes Act of 1885 — a foundational case in federal Indian-country jurisdiction still cited today.

career.title: Won Goldmark v. Kreling
career.organization: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California
career.notes: Early ruling protecting authors-composers rights; cited as legal precedent for sound-recording copyright in California prior to passage of the federal CLASSICS Act 1972.

career.title: Founder, Joseph D. Redding law firm
career.notes: Established his own firm with offices in both San Francisco and New York City.

career.title: Lead counsel, Hatzfeldt v. Huntington estate
career.notes: Represented Clara von Hatzfeldt in a contested-will case against the estate of Collis P. Huntington (railroad magnate). Won her a $6 million judgment; his $300,000 fee was at the time the highest amount of money earned by a lawyer in a court case involving only individual people (vs. corporations).

career.title: Pacific Coast Chess Champion
career.organization: Mercantile Library Association of San Francisco
career.notes: Beat Johannes Zukertort twice in 1884 (a blindfold simul, then individual; lost a third Evans-Gambit match); won the 1888 SF chess tournament; beat master George H. D. Gossip; defeated the entire Sacramento Chess Club by telegraph in 1889. Also played Wilhelm Steinitz, Emanuel Lasker, José Raúl Capablanca. 1907 guest speaker at the Manhattan Chess Club annual dinner.

career.title: President, San Francisco Arts Administration
career.notes: Held the presidency for several years.

career.title: Member, Bohemian Club
career.notes: Served as president. Wrote 'The Cremation of Care' (Druid Jinks, 1893) — Grove's signature opening ritual since 1893. Composed/wrote 'The Man of the Forest' (1902, first formal Grove play); 'The Atonement of Pan' (1912 music-drama with Henry Hadley). Also member of the Pacific-Union Club.

career.title: Co-founder, San Francisco Symphony
career.notes: One of the founding board members 1909 of the Musical Association — the organization that established the SF Symphony in 1911. Through his advocacy his friend composer Henry Kimball Hadley was appointed the orchestra's first music director; recommended Dutch violinist Eduard Tak as founding concertmaster.

career.title: Opera librettist
career.notes: Wrote libretto for Natoma (1911) — Victor Herbert music, Met Opera Philadelphia premiere with Mary Garden + John McCormack, Andreas Dippel company toured 30+ American cities. Also librettos for Hadley's The Atonement of Pan (1912) and Semper Virens (1923).

career.title: Composer, Fay Yen Fah
career.notes: His only opera as composer; premiered Monte Carlo Opera Feb 1925 — FIRST GRAND OPERA COMPOSED BY AN AMERICAN TO HAVE ITS PREMIERE IN EUROPE. France awarded him the Legion of Honour for the achievement. Later staged by the San Francisco Opera.

career.title: Songwriter and pianist
career.notes: Hits include 'Song to Hawaii' / 'Aloha to Hawaii' (1916, popular hit); 'Sweet Peggy O'Neil' (1920 by John McCormack); 'Hebe' (1900 piano piece by Victor); 'The Winds from Over the Sea' (1929 by Johnny Noble). 'Song to Hawaii' was recorded by multiple Hawaiian musicians.

career.title: Inducted, Société académique d'histoire internationale
career.notes: French scholarly society induction.

narrative_blocks.header: Argued landmark Indian-law case at twenty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: In 1886 at age twenty-seven, Joseph argued United States v. Kagama before the United States Supreme Court, representing Kagama, a Yurok Native American accused of murder. Justice Samuel Freeman Miller's opinion upheld the constitutionality of the 1885 Major Crimes Act — a foundational federal Indian-country ruling still cited today.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote America's first opera produced in France
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph wrote the libretto for Natoma (1911), composed by Victor Herbert and starring Mary Garden, which the Andreas Dippel company toured to thirty-plus American cities. Fourteen years later, his own opera Fay Yen Fah premiered in 1925 at the Monte Carlo Opera — the first grand opera by an American to premiere in Europe.

narrative_blocks.header: France awarded him the Legion of Honour
narrative_blocks.body: France awarded Joseph the Legion of Honour for Fay Yen Fah, his 1925 grand opera. He was also inducted into the Société académique d'histoire internationale in 1919. His daughter Josephine, who died nursing in the French Croix-Rouge during World War I at twenty-six, also received the Légion d'Honneur.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded the San Francisco Symphony in 1911
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph was one of the founding board members of the 1909 Musical Association — the organization that established the San Francisco Symphony in 1911. Through his advocacy his friend Henry Kimball Hadley was appointed the orchestra's first music director, and Dutch violinist Eduard Tak was hired as founding concertmaster.

narrative_blocks.header: Pacific Coast chess champion in 1888
narrative_blocks.body: A talented amateur chess polymath, Joseph beat international master Johannes Zukertort twice in 1884, won the 1888 San Francisco tournament to become Pacific Coast champion, beat master George H. D. Gossip, and defeated the entire Sacramento Chess Club by telegraph in 1889.

narrative_blocks.header: Authored Bohemian Grove's 'Cremation of Care' ritual
narrative_blocks.body: President of the Bohemian Club and the San Francisco Arts Administration, Joseph wrote 'The Cremation of Care' — the elaborate funeral spectacle with Druid priests and chained captives that has opened the annual encampment at Bohemian Grove since 1893 — and 'The Man of the Forest,' the first formal Grove play, in 1902.

death.cause: died in sleep at home, 1000 Mason St, after seven-year illness

burial.plot_notes: Funeral Grace Cathedral, San Francisco, November 23, 1932

bio.photo_notes: Photograph shows Myra with her daughter Josephine Redding (HD3752, the WWI Croix-Rouge nurse who died age 26)

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of a Croix-Rouge nursing fatality
narrative_blocks.body: Myra's daughter Josephine Redding died in 1915 at age twenty-two while nursing in the French Red Cross during World War I. France awarded Josephine both the Médaille de Guerre and the Croix-Rouge diploma. The loss of an American niece in French wartime service marked one corner of the Cowles family's broader nineteenth-century expansion across Cleveland, Chicago, and California.

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Lawn Memorial Park, Colma CA. Inscription: 'Infirmière Auxiliaire, Union des Femmes de France, Croix Rouge, 1914-1915, Morte pour la France.'

notable.notable_blurb: WWI Croix-Rouge nurse; Légion d'Honneur, died young
notable.primary_url_label: Find a Grave: Josephine Redding (Cypress Lawn)

parents.father_name: Joseph Deighn Redding (Bohemian Club president; Natoma + Fay Yen Fah composer; Légion d'Honneur)

military_service.branch: Union des Femmes de France / French Red Cross
military_service.notes: Served with the Union des Femmes de France division of the Croix-Rouge française beginning 1914. Her tombstone bears the French inscription 'Infirmière Auxiliaire, Union des Femmes de France, Croix Rouge, 1914-1915, Morte pour la France' — the formal French phrase reserved for those who died in service to the nation.

cross_connections.display_label: WWII Iwo Jima Major cousin born after her death
WWII Red Cross Pacific cousin born after her death

narrative_blocks.header: WWI Croix-Rouge auxiliary nurse 'Morte pour la France'
narrative_blocks.body: Josephine served as an auxiliary nurse with the Union des Femmes de France division of the Croix-Rouge française beginning in 1914. Her gravestone at Cypress Lawn bears the French inscription 'Infirmière Auxiliaire, Union des Femmes de France, Croix Rouge, 1914-1915, Morte pour la France' — the formal phrase reserved for those who died in service to France.

narrative_blocks.header: Awarded the Légion d'Honneur for her war nursing
narrative_blocks.body: Josephine received the Légion d'Honneur for her work as a Croix-Rouge nurse during World War I, the same French national decoration her father Joseph would later receive in 1925 for composing Fay Yen Fah. She also earned the Croix-Rouge Médaille de Guerre.

narrative_blocks.header: Born France; died at twenty-six in New York
narrative_blocks.body: Born February 25, 1893 in France during her parents' European years, Josephine died in New York on October 7, 1919 at age twenty-six — only daughter of San Francisco lawyer-composer Joseph Deighn Redding and Myra Cowles Redding. She was buried with her Cowles grandparents at Cypress Lawn in Colma.

burial.plot_notes: Sacramento Historic City Cemetery; main headstone in the Redding family plot — one of four names etched into the towering headstone (with wife Mary Prescott Putnam Redding and son George H. Redding)

notable.notable_blurb: California Secretary of State; Sacramento mayor

education.notes: Educated at Yarmouth Academy, Nova Scotia, before emigrating to Boston in 1840 at age 16.

career.title: Boston clerk
career.notes: Worked as a clerk in Boston after immigrating from Nova Scotia at age 16.

career.title: Retail grocery and ship chandlery owner
career.notes: Boston-area grocery and ship chandlery business in the 1840s.

career.title: Gold rush miner
career.notes: Joined California Gold Rush; mined the Yuba River diggings and Pittsburg bar in Yuba/Sierra counties.

career.title: Member, California State Assembly
career.notes: Elected from Yuba and Sierra counties. During session also wrote for the San Joaquin Republican and Sacramento's Democratic State Journal.

career.title: Editor and proprietor, Democratic State Journal
career.notes: Sacramento newspaper editor; previously associate editor of the Shasta Journal.

career.title: Mayor of Sacramento
career.notes: Elected mayor of California's capital city.

career.title: Secretary of State of California
career.notes: Appointed by Governor Frederick Low. Left state government in 1868 with administration change.

career.title: Land Agent, Central Pacific Railroad
career.notes: First land agent for the Central Pacific Railroad. In 1868 bought property in 'Poverty Flats' on behalf of the railroad as a northern terminus. When the line reached Shasta County in summer 1872, the railroad named the new town REDDING, CALIFORNIA in his honor.

career.title: Regent, University of California
career.notes: Appointed to fill the unexpired term of Regent Frank M. Pixley 1880-1882; reappointed 1882.

career.title: California Fish Commissioner
career.organization: California State Board of Fish Commissioners
career.notes: Held the office at the time of his death in 1882.

career.title: Member, California Academy of Sciences
career.notes: Member; especially interested in coastal paleontology. Collected and donated numerous prehistoric and aboriginal relics to the Academy's museum. Also member of the Geographical Society of the Pacific.

narrative_blocks.header: Has a California town named for him
narrative_blocks.body: As the first land agent for the Central Pacific Railroad, Benjamin bought property at 'Poverty Flats' in 1868 to serve as the railroad's northern terminus. When the tracks reached Shasta County in summer 1872, the railroad named the new town Redding in his honor — today a Sacramento Valley city of nearly ninety thousand.

narrative_blocks.header: California Secretary of State 1863-1867
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed by Governor Frederick Low, Benjamin served as California's Secretary of State from 1863 to 1867. Earlier he had represented Yuba and Sierra counties in the California State Assembly 1853-1854 and was elected mayor of Sacramento in 1856 during the gold rush boom.

narrative_blocks.header: Coastal paleontologist and aboriginal-relics collector
narrative_blocks.body: A member of both the California Academy of Sciences and the Geographical Society of the Pacific, Benjamin was especially interested in coastal paleontology. He collected numerous prehistoric and aboriginal relics, which he presented to the Academy's museum. He also served as California Fish Commissioner.

narrative_blocks.header: From Nova Scotia clerk to California gold-rusher
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Yarmouth Nova Scotia in 1824, Benjamin emigrated alone to Boston at sixteen as a clerk, then ran a grocery and ship chandlery business in the 1840s. Joining the California Gold Rush in 1850, he mined the Yuba River and Pittsburg bar before pivoting to journalism, politics, and the railroad.

sources.short: Exploring Shasta County History (Jeremy M. Tuggle 2019)

narrative_blocks.header: Descended from Revolutionary War general Israel Putnam
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's family traced descent from American Revolutionary War general Israel Putnam (1718-1790), one of the most colorful officers of the Continental Army. The Putnam line passed through Mary to her son Joseph Deighn Redding, who later became a prominent San Francisco lawyer-composer and married into the Hooker tree.

narrative_blocks.header: Joined her husband in gold-rush California
narrative_blocks.body: Mary remained in Massachusetts with their first two sons William and J. Albert while Benjamin sailed for the 1849 California gold rush. After Benjamin established a settled California life as journalist, mayor of Sacramento, and California Secretary of State, Mary and the boys joined him; their younger sons George and Joseph were both born in California.

sources.short: Wikipedia: Joseph Redding (passing reference to mother's Putnam line)

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.
